claims.claim_text: The war story claims the fraud model achieved 96% accuracy before deployment.
claims.span_hint: line 283 | section: War Story: The Model That Worked Until It Didn't
claims.suggested_rewrite: It reached strong offline metrics in testing.
claims.rationale: This is a precise quantitative anecdote with no attributable source, so the fake-specific number should be removed rather than hedged.

claims.claim_text: The war story claims fraud losses tripled three months after deployment.
claims.span_hint: line 289 | section: War Story: The Model That Worked Until It Didn't
claims.anchor_text: Three months later, fraud losses tripled.
claims.suggested_rewrite: Later, fraud losses rose sharply enough for the team to notice a business problem.
claims.rationale: This is an unsupported pseudo-incident with a precise time and impact multiplier, so the false specifics should be collapsed to the operational lesson.

claims.claim_text: Most organizations are at Level 0 or Level 1 of MLOps maturity.
claims.anchor_text: Most organizations are at Level 0 or Level 1.
claims.suggested_rewrite: Many organizations begin at Level 0 or Level 1.
claims.rationale: The module states a majority/prevalence claim without a cited survey or dataset, so the unsupported quantifier should be removed.

claims.claim_text: A team might use DVC for data versioning, MLflow for experiment tracking and registry, GitHub Actions for pipeline automation, KServe for serving, and Evidently for drift checks.
claims.span_hint: line 1274 | section: The MLOps Tool Landscape
claims.anchor_text: For example, a team might use DVC for data versioning, MLflow for experiment tracking and registry, GitHub Actions for pipeline automation, KServe for Kubernetes serving, and Evidently for drift checks.
claims.suggested_rewrite: For example, a team might combine separate tools for data versioning, experiment tracking, pipeline automation, model serving, and drift checks.
claims.rationale: This is an illustrative stack sketch spanning several products, some of whose primary docs are off-allowlist, so a generalized example preserves the lesson without forcing mixed-product inline citations.

claims.claim_text: Kubernetes Jobs are a run-to-completion primitive and are a natural fit for one-off or batch ML training workloads.
claims.rationale: The Kubernetes Jobs documentation explicitly defines Jobs as one-off tasks that run to completion and even includes a distributed-training example.

claims.claim_text: A Kubernetes-based MLOps setup may use Argo Workflows or Kubeflow Pipelines for orchestration.
claims.anchor_text: It may use Argo Workflows or Kubeflow Pipelines for orchestration.
claims.suggested_rewrite: It may use a workflow orchestrator to coordinate ML pipeline steps.
claims.rationale: This is an illustrative tool-choice sentence, and softening avoids attaching one citation to a mixed on/off-allowlist product pair.

claims.claim_text: A Kubernetes-based MLOps setup may use KServe or Seldon for inference.
claims.anchor_text: It may use KServe or Seldon for inference.
claims.suggested_rewrite: It may use a model-serving layer for inference.
claims.rationale: This sentence is serving-tool illustration rather than a load-bearing product comparison, so a generalized rewrite is cleaner than forcing unsupported mixed-product anchoring.

claims.claim_text: A Kubernetes-based MLOps setup may use Prometheus and Grafana for infrastructure metrics.
claims.anchor_text: It may use Prometheus and Grafana for infrastructure metrics.
claims.suggested_rewrite: It may use standard observability tooling for infrastructure metrics.
claims.rationale: The lesson is the need for infrastructure observability, not these exact product names, so a generic rewrite is sufficient.

claims.claim_text: Most ML projects struggle to reach production because operational gaps outlast modeling work.
claims.anchor_text: - **Most ML projects struggle to reach production because operational gaps outlast modeling work**: deployment, monitoring, ownership, and reproducibility often become the limiting factors.
claims.suggested_rewrite: - **Operational gaps often become the limiting factor in getting ML systems to production**: deployment, monitoring, ownership, and reproducibility can outlast the modeling work.
claims.rationale: This asserts an uncited majority outcome about ML projects as a whole, so the unsupported prevalence claim should be rewritten as a non-quantified observation.

claims.claim_text: ML-specific issues such as training-serving skew and drift can degrade predictions without ordinary service errors, so models can fail silently if they are not properly monitored.
claims.rationale: Google's predictive-ML quality guide explicitly says deployed model performance can degrade over time, can fail silently if not monitored, and can be harmed by training-serving skew and drift.

claims.claim_text: Experiment tracking logs run metadata so teams can compare experiments and reproduce results later.
claims.rationale: The MLflow Tracking docs directly describe logging parameters, code versions, metrics, and output files for later analysis and comparison, but mlflow.org is off the current allowlist.

further_reading.title: MLOps: Continuous delivery and automation pipelines in machine learning
further_reading.why_relevant: This is the strongest high-level reference for the module's maturity model, pipeline automation, and staged adoption framing.

further_reading.title: Guidelines for developing high-quality, predictive ML solutions
further_reading.why_relevant: It directly covers silent failure, training-serving skew, drift, monitoring, validation, and production-quality controls for ML systems.

further_reading.title: Kubernetes Jobs
further_reading.why_relevant: This is the canonical reference for the run-to-completion workload primitive used in the Kubernetes-and-MLOps section.

further_reading.title: Argo Workflows
further_reading.why_relevant: It is a concrete example of the workflow-orchestrator role mentioned in the Kubernetes-based MLOps stack discussion.

notes: No shared citation pool appears to exist for this module, so `section_pool_ref` is null and `source_ids` are empty. I skipped Mermaid diagrams, code fences, quiz items and answers, and hands-on exercise steps per instructions. Several tool-name example lines mix products whose best primary docs are split across allowlisted and off-allowlist domains (for example MLflow, Kubeflow, KServe), so I favored illustrative rewrites there instead of forcing weak or mismatched citations.
